replacement and capacity project.
So we had two projects listed in Schedule two
of the Fast Tracked Approvals Act,
but only one of them seems to have carried through into the,
um, the, into the bill.
And, um, for reasons that are unclear to us, the, um, our,
our high voltage direct current cable replacement project
has been left off, um, schedule two A in the bill.
So we are seeking that, that be, that'd be reinstated
because we, um, we do see a potential need
to utilize the Public Works Act for, for that project.
Um, we also just note that, um, we have heard, um,
some submissions to this committee, uh, on Friday
and earlier today expressing concerns around Maori land.
Um, so we wanted to, to put forward that, um, you know,
trans power avoids Maori land wherever possible.
We go through a detailed route selection,
site selection process when, um, identifying, um, the land
that we, we need and, uh, we, we go to every length possible
to avoid Maori land.
Um, also noting there is, there is case law, um,
that Maori land should only be considered
as an absolute last resort as well.
Um, anything further to add to that? Okay.
I think we can hand over
for questions in the time we've got.
Okay, thank you, cam. Uh, thank you very much.
You, me, made a mention about, um, using easements, uh,
mostly instead of public works.
How did, how do you come to a figure for compensation
around the use of easements?
Yeah, um, yes we do, we do use easements.
Um, we, we sometimes do also acquire freehold and, um,
and easements can be acquired under the Public Works Act,
so it's just a lesser interest in land as freehold.
Um, we have those, um, the compensation assessed
by a registered valuer
and the landowner will have a registered valuer
and we will, um, procure services of a,
of a registered valuer
and we will then negotiate a, um, a settlement amount.
Um, that's how we currently acquire easements.
If we had to acquire under the Public Works Act, we would've
to work under the compensation methodology under the Public
Works Act, which is more regimented.
And one, uh, my question
before getting to t uh, the $30 million, where,
how did you arrive at that number as a figure?
And is there gonna be any kind of, if it was
to be adopted into the bill, any kind of, uh,
inflation adjusting or any
suggestion you have along those lines?
Basically, how did you come up with 30 million?
I can answer that question that that is the, um,
threshold under the commerce, um, commission regime
for trans power for major CapEx projects.
So if it's an enhancement
or a new line, that is the
threshold for going down that route.
Cool. Thank you Tangi.
Yeah, thank you Mr. Chair.
Um, thank you trans power for your submission.
Um, you are right in that a lot of the awareness
of projects in this bill are largely transport related,
related ones, and that means that they are all listed
your proposal to basically have your infrastructure,
you know, threshold of above 30 million.
And the mechanism being an order in council provides less
scrutiny than those projects
that are listed in the schedule.
So why do you, you know, why do you think that there is a,
an appropriate difference between that as a mechanism?
I think trans power would be happy providing a list
of projects if they're provided more comfort.
The list that we've given is, is projects
that we envisage coming up.
I guess unlike Waka Kotahi,
we don't benefit from a government policy statement,
but certainly we weren't anticipating that there would be
that, that the avenue we've proposed
would result in no discretion.
We certainly expect that there would be some as
to which projects could, could be listed.
No, and I think Just to add to that,
I guess there's sort of scrutiny through other processes.
Anything above 30 million needs to go
through a commerce commission approval process.
There's scrutiny through that process, um, and consultation
and likewise through RMA processes.
No, I, I guess my issue is
that the public have an opportunity to scrutinize this list,
but what you are suggesting is utilizing a,
a secondary legislation process that what,
like the public won't have an opportunity
to scrutinize that.
Um, do you see the, the point of difference there?
I mean, I think what you're saying is
that they won't have an opportunity to scrutinize the list,
but there is an opportunity to scrutinize each project
through other mechanisms is what is what we're suggesting.
Okay. Thank you. Thank you.
Um, can I just ask one question?
We, we, we've got time,
but, um, uh, we are slightly ahead overall, um,
just in terms of the decision making process.
Um, and one of the things we've had trans power in front
of us several times before, um, stressing
how important the relationships are that are formed, uh,
along the roots where you have transmission lines.
Um, now that's been, that's been spelled out to us time
and time again by trans power.
When you go down this process, uh,
and I know it's, it's been a frus it's often a frustrating
and lengthy process, but if you go down this process,
how do you see that relationship management working?
Because if you've got lead easements,
you're still on somebody else's easements over somebody
else's land with your, you know,
you pile on in the middle of the land, et cetera.
How does that work?
I mean, uh, you, uh, you're absolutely right Andy,
that we have, um, you know, it's very important for us
to have good relationships with landowners, um,
on an enduring basis.
And that is something that we've
talked to this committee about.
I mean, I think, um, you know, we would,
our pre our strong kind of plan a preference is to acquire,
um, property rights voluntarily.
And that, you know, we would, we've always taken
that approach, um, and we would continue to do so.
Um, but we're also conscious with the pace
of electrification
and the need to get work done, um, more promptly
and quickly, that from time to time we may need recourse to,
um, public Works Act legislation.
So hence we have an interest that if we have to use
that plan B mechanism, um, that it works,
uh, effectively for us.
Okay. And, and look, um, that's, that's helpful.
Um, yeah. Okay. No, that's me. Uh, anybody else?
Any other questions? Look, thank you very much.
Really appreciate, uh, the submission and, um,
and uh, coming before us and, uh, answering some questions.
Thank you. Thanks
Very much, team.
Thanks.
Yeah, private.
So now we go into the private zoom.
So if you log off and log.

source_metadata.title: 2025/06/23 - TI
source_metadata.description:
Monday 23 June 2025 | Transport and Infrastructure Committee

Public Works (Critical Infrastructure) Amendment Bill

Hearing of evidence:

Māori Women's Welfare League (Dr Hope Tupara, National President)
Sharon Campbell
Environment and Conservation Organisations of NZ Aotearoa (Cath Wallace, Vice Chairperson)
Transpower New Zealand Limited (Matt Fanning, Head of Land Owner Relations and Property; Raewyn Moss, Executive General Manager, Customers & External Affairs)


For other hearings on this bill see parts 3 and 5 of 2025/06/19
